# Background information

[ORAI1](https://www.uniprot.org/uniprotkb/Q96D31/entry) is the essential pore-forming subunit of the Store-Operated Calcium (SOC) channel, specifically the Calcium Release-Activated Calcium (CRAC) channel @Yeung2020. Located in the plasma membrane, it mediates highly selective Ca2+ influx triggered by the depletion of endoplasmic reticulum calcium stores. This influx is vital for diverse cellular processes, including gene transcription and exocytosis@Derler2012.

Structurally, each human ORAI1 subunit consists of four transmembrane domains connected by two extracellular loops, one intracellular loop, and flanqued by two cytosolic strands (N- and C- terminus) [@Derler2012; @Tiffner2021]. The functional ORAI1 channel operates as a hexameric complex, as its biological activity depends on the precise assembly of multiple subunits rather than the individual monomer @Tiffner2021. The central pore is formed by six TM1 domains and it is indispensable for the gating mechanism and overall stability of the Orai1 channel.

While ORAI1 is the primary focus of this study, it belongs to a family of three isoforms (ORAI1–3) that share a conserved topology of four transmembrane (TM) domains but exhibit distinct regulatory profiles @Tiffner2021.
The ORAI1 homohexamer is characterized by high Ca2+ selectivity and robust ion influx; however, the functional channel in vivo often exists as a heteromeric complex. For instance, ORAI1/ORAI3 heteropentamers or ORAI1/ORAI2 heterohexamers significantly alter calcium sensitivity and inactivation rates compared to the homomeric form @Derler2012. These stoichiometric variations suggest that the optimal modeling target must be carefully selected based on the specific physiological context.

The biological activity of ORAI1 is strictly dependent on its physical coupling with STIM1, an endoplasmic reticulum (ER) calcium sensor. At resting state, STIM1 is uniformly distributed within the ER membrane; however, upon store depletion, it undergoes a conformational change to interact directly with ORAI1 @Derler2012. Both the N- and C-terminal cytosolic extensions of ORAI1 are essential for this process, acting as the primary docking sites. his physical coupling stabilizes the hexameric assembly and 'forces' the conformational change required to open the pore.

The functional state of the channel is heavily influenced by specific chemical environments and molecular partners that should be accounted for in any simulation. For example, terminal regions bind Calmodulin, which mediates calcium-dependent inactivation @Derler2012. Additionaly, cholesterol acts as a negative regulator of ORAI1 becase it suppresses CRAC channel activity @Derler2016.

The extracellular loops of ORAI1 provide critical regulatory sites: the first loop (TM1-TM2) facilitates electrostatic Ca2+ attraction, while the third loop contains the sole glycosylation site at N223. This cell-type-specific modification enables interaction with lectins that attenuate store-operated calcium entry @Humer2022. Additionally, cysteine residues at the domain interfaces (C126, C143, and C195) mediate redox regulation, where H2O2 exposure imposes a state-dependent inhibitory effect @Humer2022.

Finally, it is essential to note that native cells express two distinct variants of the Orai1 protein: alpha and beta. The alpha isoform is the full-length, canonical version of the protein. In contrast, the beta isoform lacks the long N-terminal domain (residues 1-63) @Fukushima2012. Due to its complete regulatory sequence and role in both channel types, the alpha isoform is utilized as the template for the structural modeling in this study.
